Title: The Regulation of Essential Amino Acid Synthesis and Accumulation in Plants

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: high ADT1 expression positively correlates with endogenous Phe biosynthesis in flowers
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADT1 expression is extremely high in petunia petals. 2. High expression positively correlates with endogenous Phe biosynthesis in flowers.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: expression analysis and correlation with metabolite production
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In this review, ADT1 is highlighted as a key summarized gene but not via direct transgenic validation in the review text.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: expression in Arabidopsis increases phenylalanine content
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Recombinant bacterial PheA expressed in Arabidopsis increased Phe content. 2. This demonstrated that plants can convert prephenate into Phe through an additional route.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: mediates conversion linking Tyr catabolism with cytosolic Phe production
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Molecular identification showed that cytosolic Tyr:phenylpyruvate aminotransferase mediates the relevant conversion in the phenylpyruvate route. 2. This links Tyr catabolism and plastidial aromatic amino acid biosynthesis to downstream pathways.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: encodes functional histidinol-phosphate phosphatase in the final step of His biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The final enzyme of plant His biosynthesis was identified as myoinositol monophosphatase-like 2. 2. It was shown to encode a functional histidinol-phosphate phosphatase.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: dramatically upregulated after a range of stresses and hormone treatments
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: identified as causal for BCAA variation in Arabidopsis seeds
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BCAT2 is plastid localized and largely biosynthetic in Arabidopsis. 2. It is strongly induced by stresses and hormones. 3. Genome-wide association mapping identified BCAT2 as causal for BCAAs in Arabidopsis seeds.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: participates in BCAA biosynthesis; in Arabidopsis also linked to glucosinolate biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BCAT3 is plastidial and contributes to BCAA biosynthesis. 2. In Arabidopsis it also participates in glucosinolate biosynthesis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis BCAT4 shows unusual substrate specificity and mutants slightly reduce Leu; tomato BCAT4 overexpression enhances BCAA levels
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis BCAT4 is plastidial with unusual substrate specificity and only slight Leu reduction in knockout mutants. 2. In tomato, BCAT4 is plastidial and overexpression elevates BCAA levels, consistent with a causal QTL.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: knockout mutant analysis; overexpression; QTL colocalization

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Solanum lycopersicum; Spinacia oleracea; Oryza sativa
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: antisense inhibition in tomato decreases all BCAAs in leaves and fruits
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant KARI forms a homodimer and has very high affinity for Mg2+. 2. It colocalizes with BCAA QTLs in tomato. 3. Antisense inhibition lowers all BCAAs in tomato leaves and fruits.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required for BCAA biosynthesis; reverse genetics in tomato supports causal role in BCAA QTL
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DHAD is essential for BCAA biosynthesis. 2. Tomato reverse genetics confirmed it as a possible causal QTL for BCAA content and showed effects on other amino acids and sugars.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: genuine isopropylmalate synthase with compensatory redundancy for Leu biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAM2 encodes a genuine isopropylmalate synthase. 2. MAM2 and MAM4 have nearly identical substrate specificities. 3. Lack of Leu change in mutants suggests compensation among isoforms.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: genuine isopropylmalate synthase with compensatory redundancy for Leu biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAM4 encodes a genuine isopropylmalate synthase. 2. MAM2 and MAM4 have nearly identical substrate specificities. 3. Lack of Leu change in mutants suggests compensation among isoforms.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Pisum sativum
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: inactivation causes major accumulation of BCAAs and changes in glucosinolates; functions in BCAA and Lys degradation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase is relatively well characterized in several species. 2. Arabidopsis mutants lacking its activity accumulate all three BCAAs strongly and alter glucosinolates. 3. It functions in both BCAA degradation and Lys breakdown.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: supports alternative amino acid catabolic flux into the mitochondrial electron transfer flavoprotein system
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 2-D-hydroxyglutarate dehydrogenase feeds electrons from aromatic amino acid and especially Lys catabolism into the ETF pathway. 2. Feeding experiments suggest Lys can be degraded through a 2-hydroxyglutarate route in plants.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: feeding experiments; mutant analysis; metabolic flux interpretation

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required for mitochondrial Leu catabolism; knockout impairs reproductive growth and seed germination
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase was the first described enzyme of Leu degradation in plants. 2. Knockouts in either subunit block mitochondrial Leu catabolism. 3. Mutants show aberrant flowers/siliques and reduced seed germination.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major control step for free histidine content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes histidine accumulation; overexpression of the first enzyme exerts the greatest control over free histidine levels
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. All nine His-biosynthetic enzymes were identified in Arabidopsis. 2. Overexpression analysis showed that most control of free His content resides in ATP-phosphoribosyltransferase. 3. Both ATP-phosphoribosyltransferase isoforms are feedback regulated by histidine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: individual enzyme overexpression analysis; mutant analysis; pathway characterization
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis; enzyme active-site chemistry; metal coordination; nickel binding/tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; summarizes evidence rather than a single new experiment.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Aspartate-family amino acid biosynthesis; lysine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: first unique enzyme of lysine synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key regulatory step; major feedback-regulated control point
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: feedback-insensitive DHDPS increases lysine accumulation strongly, especially in seeds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The first Lys-accumulating mutant was a feedback-insensitive form of DHDPS. 2. Overexpression of bacterial feedback-insensitive DHDPS significantly elevated Lys. 3. Combining seed-specific DHDPS expression with loss of Lys catabolism caused nearly 64-fold free Lys increase in Arabidopsis seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: feedback-insensitive mutant analysis; transgenic overexpression; seed-specific expression; metabolite profiling; genetic combination with catabolic knockout
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: condenses aspartate-semialdehyde with pyruvate to form dihydrodipicolinate in the lysine branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: committed unique step supplying lysine branch flux
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Often described in the literature as DHDDS/DHDPS in this review context.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: important regulatory step limiting/regulating threonine and contributing to lysine/methionine flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: feedback-insensitive AK causes significant threonine accumulation and can increase methionine when combined with deregulated CGS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AK isoforms are feedback inhibited by Lys or Thr. 2. Mutants and transgenic plants with feedback-insensitive AK accumulate Thr. 3. AK expression is light regulated and responds to sucrose and inorganic phosphate. 4. Coexpression with feedback-insensitive CGS enhances Met accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: feedback-insensitive mutant analysis; bacterial overexpression; transgenic coexpression studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: light regulated; regulated by photosynthesis-related metabolites, sucrose, and inorganic phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: phosphorylates aspartate to initiate the Asp-family pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for Lys/Thr/Met/Ile biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: aspartate kinase family; mono- or bifunctional forms with HSD in plants
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light; sucrose; inorganic phosphate; abiotic stress
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core pathway control enzyme across multiple essential amino acid branches.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: main catabolic control point used to elevate lysine by suppression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: loss or suppression reduces lysine degradation and strongly increases free lysine accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LKR/SDH converts Lys into Glu and α-aminoadipic semialdehyde through the saccharopine pathway. 2. Knockout or RNAi suppression of LKR/SDH elevates Lys in Arabidopsis and maize. 3. Combining catabolic suppression with deregulated biosynthesis gives very large Lys increases.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: radiolabeled Lys feeding; knockout; RNAi suppression; transgenic stacking with DHDPS
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: catalyzes initial lysine catabolism via saccharopine pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target metabolite accumulation by degrading lysine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A major target for lysine biofortification.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Aspartate-family amino acid biosynthesis; threonine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: last step of threonine synthesis; branch point with methionine pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: modulates Thr/Met partitioning; reduced TS increases Met, while antisense causes growth retardation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TS is allosterically regulated by SAM. 2. As a branch-point enzyme, TS influences partitioning between Thr and Met biosynthesis. 3. Antisense TS plants show significant growth retardation, and reduced TS activity increases Met content strongly.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: enzyme crystallization with/without SAM; antisense suppression; mutant analysis; heterologous overexpression
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms threonine and competes with methionine branch for O-phosphohomoserine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Central branch regulator connecting Thr and Met synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THA1 encodes a Thr catabolic enzyme converting Thr to Gly and acetaldehyde. 2. THA1 complements yeast gly1 sbm1 sbm2 mutant. 3. THA1 mutation leads to greatly enhanced Thr content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: yeast complementation; mutant analysis; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts threonine to glycine and acetaldehyde
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target metabolite accumulation by degrading threonine

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: THA2 mutation is embryo lethal, likely due to Thr overaccumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THA2 is a second Thr aldolase gene in Arabidopsis. 2. THA2 complements yeast gly1 sbm1 sbm2 mutant. 3. THA2 mutation causes embryo lethality, likely caused by Thr overaccumulation rather than Gly deficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: yeast complementation; mutant analysis; chemical/genetic complementation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts threonine to glycine and acetaldehyde
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target metabolite accumulation by degrading threonine

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; tobacco; potato; alfalfa; soybean; azuki bean
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Solanum tuberosum; Medicago sativa; Glycine max; Vigna angularis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major regulatory enzyme for methionine accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: overexpression significantly elevates methionine; feedback-insensitive forms cause dramatic Met increases
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CGS is the first committed enzyme of Met biosynthesis and a major control point. 2. Overexpression of AtCGS raises Met in multiple species. 3. Mutations in the MTO1 domain reduce SAM-mediated feedback regulation. 4. Feedback-insensitive AtD-CGS can raise Met nearly tenfold alone and up to 173-fold when combined with feedback-insensitive AK. 5. Seed-specific AtD-CGS expression increases seed Met and total amino acids/protein.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: overexpression in multiple species; mutant analysis; domain dissection; transgene stacking; seed-specific expression; metabolomics; transcriptomics
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: combines O-phosphohomoserine with cysteine-derived sulfur to form cystathionine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: committed step directly controls methionine branch flux
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PLP-dependent enzyme with N-terminal MTO1 regulatory domain
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: sucrose; threonine; ethylene; light; environmental stress; SAM
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: reduced glutathione and lower germination under oxidative stress in high-Met seeds, but improved germination under osmotic and salt stress
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: At least one additional regulatory domain near MTO1 was reported.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: not established; reported not to play major regulatory role in Met content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: does not appear to determine methionine content strongly
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CBL participates in Met biosynthesis. 2. Sense and antisense potato studies indicate it does not play a major regulatory role in Met accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts cystathionine toward homocysteine formation in Met biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: not established; reported not to play major regulatory role in Met content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: does not appear to determine methionine content strongly
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MS catalyzes the final conversion to Met. 2. Sense and antisense potato studies indicate it does not play a major regulatory role in determining Met content.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major determinant of free methionine consumption
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: reduced expression greatly elevates Met but causes severe abnormal phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. About half of leaf Met is converted to SAM and associated metabolites. 2. Reduction of SAMS expression increases Met up to 443-fold. 3. Because SAM is central to metabolism, SAMS suppression causes severe abnormal phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: radioactive Met tracing; in silico modeling; gene suppression studies
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target metabolite accumulation by consuming methionine

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: lower MGL increases Met; overexpression promotes nematode tolerance and can compensate for loss of Thr deaminase
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MGL is a major Met catabolic enzyme. 2. Lower MGL expression elevates Met in Arabidopsis flowers/seeds and potato tubers. 3. Its expression is upregulated by combined water deficit and nematode infection. 4. Overexpression of AtMGL increases nematode tolerance. 5. MGL also contributes to aroma volatile synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene suppression; overexpression; stress-response expression analysis; metabolite analysis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: catabolizes methionine, contributing to Ile production and volatile formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target metabolite accumulation by degrading methionine
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: combined water deficit and nematode infection

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: feedback-insensitive mutants accumulate Trp to about threefold wild-type
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Anthranilate synthase is a heterotetramer of two α and two β subunits. 2. It is feedback inhibited by Trp binding to the β subunit. 3. Feedback-insensitive mutants accumulate Trp about threefold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant screening using anthranilate fluorescence; biochemical characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts chorismate to anthranilate, initiating Trp biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: committed step directly controlling Trp production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Anthranilate fluorescence under UV was used as marker for mutants.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Petunia hybrida
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: upstream feedback-sensitive control step circumvented by bacterial insensitive AroG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: expression of feedback-insensitive bacterial AroG increases shikimate, Phe, Trp, and derived secondary metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Bacterial AroG insensitive to aromatic amino acid feedback was expressed in Arabidopsis, tomato, and petunia. 2. This strongly elevated shikimate, Phe, Trp, and many derived secondary metabolites. 3. The work shows that bypassing feedback regulation is effective for engineering aromatic amino acids.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: condenses phosphoenolpyruvate and erythrose 4-phosphate to form DAHP at the entry to shikimate pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: useful metabolic engineering gene to elevate aromatic amino acids when feedback regulation is circumvented
